turns.content: ok so i'm stress-eating leftover thanksgiving food and simultaneously trying to plan out my research timeline for the next six months and it's not going well lol

Classic combination — nothing like mashed potatoes and existential deadline dread. What's the timeline looking like? Is there a hard deadline driving the stress or more of a self-imposed "I should have this figured out by now" situation?
hard deadline. trying to get this paper ready for NeurIPS, deadline is early June. which sounds like a lot of time but i have SO much experimental work left to do and the writing is basically zero rn

Early June NeurIPS — so you're looking at roughly six months, which is actually workable if you plan it well. But I get why it feels tight with experiments still running and a blank doc staring at you. What's the paper on, roughly? And where are the experiments at — are you still exploring or do you have a core result you're building around?

it's on memory systems for LLMs — like how you structure episodic vs semantic memory retrieval and the tradeoffs. we have some promising results on the retrieval side but the ablations are incomplete and tbh i'm not sure the story is tight enough yet. like i know what we found but i don't know what we're CLAIMING yet if that makes sense

That makes complete sense — and honestly that's a really common place to be at this stage. You have results but the "so what" framing isn't locked in yet. That's actually a useful diagnosis because it tells you what needs to happen before you can write efficiently: the claim has to come first, otherwise you're writing without a thesis and it shows.

One approach that works well: try writing the abstract and introduction *now*, even though it feels wrong to do that before the experiments are done. Not to publish it — just as a forcing function to articulate what you're claiming. If you can't write a clean one-paragraph version of your contribution, that's a signal about where the thinking needs work, not where the writing needs work.

For the timeline itself — six months to NeurIPS with incomplete ablations, how many more experiments are we talking? Weeks of compute or more like weeks of your own time designing/analyzing?

probably 3-4 weeks of actual compute time if i queue things smartly, but like 6-8 weeks of MY time because i have to analyze as results come in and iterate. ugh. and i have other work stuff too, it's not like i can just disappear into paper-writing mode

Right, so realistically you're looking at something like: January through February for the bulk of experimental iteration, March to nail down the story and get a full draft, April for polishing and internal review, May as a buffer/rewrite window before the June deadline. That's tight but not insane if you protect those blocks.

The tricky part is the "other work stuff" bleeding in. Is there any way to make the paper work more officially your primary focus for a chunk of that period — like actually blocking it with your manager or team — or is this more of a side project that has to fit around your regular job scope?

i mean it's technically aligned with what we do on the memory team so it's not totally a side thing, but it's also not like i have dedicated paper-writing time carved out. i'm going to have to fight for it a little. my manager is supportive in theory but in practice there's always fires to put out

Yeah, "supportive in theory" is doing a lot of work there. If you want that time protected you'll probably need to get specific with her — like actually putting "paper work: 2 blocks/week" on the calendar and treating it as real meetings, not aspirational free time. The fires fill whatever space you leave open.

One concrete thing: is there a point in your timeline where you could say "I need 2-3 weeks where I'm mostly heads-down on this"? Even getting one focused sprint agreed on in advance is better than trying to carve out an hour here and there indefinitely. What does your next month look like — is December a write-off because of holidays or is it actually quieter for you?

december is weirdly quiet because a lot of people are off so there's fewer meetings lol. might actually be the best time to make progress on the ablations. ok you're making me feel slightly less doomed about this

December low-meeting mode is genuinely a gift — lean into it hard. Queue up everything you can on the compute side before the holidays hit and use the actual quiet weeks to run analysis and iterate. If you can come out of December with the ablations substantially done and a working hypothesis about your core claim, you'll be in a much stronger position in January.

For what it's worth, the place you're describing — real results, incomplete ablations, fuzzy story — is pretty normal for a good paper at six months out. The papers that come together well at NeurIPS are usually ones where someone forced themselves to write the ugly first draft early. Even if it's bad. Especially if it's bad.

What's the biggest open question on the ablation side — the thing that would most change your story if it came out differently than you expect?

honestly whether the retrieval gains hold up when you scale the episodic memory store. right now everything is on smaller benchmarks and we haven't tested at like 10x the context. if it degrades weirdly at scale that's a problem

That's the right thing to be worried about — and also the thing to run first, before anything else. Scaling behavior has a way of either validating your story or completely reshaping it, and you don't want to be building the rest of your narrative on a result that only holds in the small regime.

Is there a version of the scaling experiment you could run at lower cost to get a directional signal quickly, before committing to the full 10x compute? Even a 3x or 5x run that gives you some slope data would let you know whether you're looking at graceful degradation (still a good story with caveats), sudden cliff behavior (needs explaining), or actually better-than-expected results (nice). Getting that signal in the next few weeks seems like it should probably be experiment priority number one.
yeah ok that's a really good point. run the mid-scale thing first to derisk the big one. i think i've been avoiding it because i'm scared of what it'll show lol but that's dumb

Ha — avoidance of the scary experiment is extremely relatable and also extremely counterproductive. But yes, you want to find out now if there's a problem, not in March. The earlier you know, the more options you have: reframe the contribution, add a section about the limitations with an explanation, run additional experiments to address it. Finding out in May is a crisis. Finding out in December is just information.

You've got a solid plan coming together: run the mid-scale ablation in December, use the quiet weeks to make serious experimental progress, come up with a draft claim statement even if it's rough. You're not as far behind as the stress eating suggested.
lmao fair point. ok this was actually really helpful, i feel like i have an actual plan now instead of just vague dread. back to the sweet potato casserole i guess

Earned it. Sweet potato casserole as a reward for facing your scary scaling experiment — that's a reasonable trade. Good luck with the ablations, and I hope December is as gloriously meeting-free as it sounds.
